title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

texts.A: The entry of private corporations into the space sector is often framed as a bold new frontier, but it is essentially a rebranding of old-fashioned vanity. [37]While some argue that private competition will lower the cost of access to orbit, I believe it will lead to a 'wild west' scenario where the celestial commons are carved up for profit without any international oversight.[/37] [39]Furthermore, the carbon footprint of recreational space travel is an environmental catastrophe that we cannot afford when our own planet is in crisis.[/39] Space should be the domain of science and diplomacy, not a playground for the ultra-wealthy.
texts.B: The efficiency of private space companies compared to government bureaucracies is undeniable. By utilizing reusable rockets and rapid prototyping, firms like SpaceX have done in a decade what NASA struggled to do in forty years. [38]I disagree with the notion that this is a zero-sum game; the innovations developed for Mars missions will inevitably lead to technologies that benefit life on Earth, from better satellite communications to new energy sources.[/38] [40]However, I share the concern that we are moving toward a dangerous legal vacuum regarding resource extraction on the moon and asteroids.[/40] We need a new 'Outer Space Treaty' that reflects the realities of the 21st century.
texts.C: The commercialization of space is not just inevitable; it is desirable. For too long, our cosmic ambitions have been at the mercy of shifting political winds and annual budgets. Private investment provides the long-term stability needed for permanent human settlements. [37]I share the view that the market will drive down costs, eventually making space travel accessible to the many, not just the elite.[/37] [38]Unlike those who see space as a distraction from Earth's problems, I believe the survival of the human species depends on our becoming a multi-planetary species.[/38] If we stay on one planet, we are essentially waiting for an extinction event.
texts.D: The hype surrounding 'space tourism' is a dangerous distraction from the real value of the private space sector: the delivery of infrastructure. The focus on billionaires in suits ignores the thousands of engineers working on small-satellite constellations that could bring internet to every corner of the globe. [39]However, I am more pessimistic than others about the environmental impact; the sheer number of launches required for these constellations threatens to pollute the upper atmosphere in ways we don't yet understand.[/39] [40]Regarding the law, I am less convinced that we need a new treaty; the existing UN framework is robust enough, provided it is actually enforced.[/40] The challenge is political will, not a lack of rules.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the likely economic outcome of private competition in space?
questions.explanation: C believes that market forces will 'drive down costs' and make space 'accessible to the many.' A is skeptical of these cost-lowering claims and focuses instead on the 'wild west' scenario of profit-seeking.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's view that space exploration is fundamentally linked to the improvement or survival of life on Earth?
questions.explanation: B argues that space missions lead to 'technologies that benefit life on Earth.' C shares this link, arguing that the 'survival of the human species' depends on becoming multi-planetary.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the ecological consequences of increased space activity?
questions.explanation: A calls the carbon footprint of space travel an 'environmental catastrophe.' D is also 'pessimistic... about the environmental impact,' specifically mentioning the pollution of the upper atmosphere.

questions.question: Which expert has a different opinion to B on the adequacy of the current legal framework for space?
questions.explanation: B believes we are in a 'legal vacuum' and need a new treaty. D disagrees, stating the 'existing UN framework is robust enough' and that a new treaty isn't necessary.
